Atiyah 第五章习题解答

Solutions to the exercises of Introduction to Commutative Algebra, Atiyah, Chapter 5.

Integral Dependence and Valuations

Exercise 1. Let f:A→B be an integral homomorphism of rings. Show that f∗:\discretionarySpec(B)→Spec(A) is a closed mapping, i.e. that it maps closed sets to closed sets. (This is a geometrical equivalent of (5.10)).

Proof. For the case when f is injective, i.e. A is a subring of B: For any p⊂A and q⊂B prime ideals, if p⊇q∩A, then by (5.11), there exists a prime ideal q′⊇q such that p=q′∩A. i.e. p∈f∗(V(p)). Conversely if p∈f∗(V(q)) then p⊇q∩A. Hence f∗(V(p))=V(p∩A), so f∗ is closed.

In the general case, f∗ is the composition of π∗ and i∗, where π:A/ker(f),i:f(A)→B. Obviously π∗ is closed, and i∗ is closed since B is integral over f(A). Thus f∗ is closed.

□

Exercise 2. Let A be a subring of a ring B such that B is integral over A, and let f:A→Ω be a homomorphism of A into an algebraically closed field Ω. Show that f can be extended to a homomorphism of B into Ω.

Proof. Let p=ker(f). Since f(A)⊂Ω is a integral domain, p is prime. By (5.10), there exists q⊂B prime such that p=q∩A. Let A1​=A/p and B1​=B/q, then B1​ is integral over A1​; and f induces an embedding of A1​ into Ω. Hence we can assume A,B are integral domains and f is injective.

Let Σ be the set (C,σ) such that A⊆C⊆B is a subring, and σ:C→Ω is an embedding, where σ∣A​=f. Define a partial order over Σ by (C,σ)≤(C′,σ′) if C⊆C′ and σ′∣C​=σ. Clear the conditions of Zorn’s lemma are satisfied, and therefore Σ has a maximal element, denote by (C,σ). We must show C=B.

Suppose for a contradiction, then there exists b∈B−C. Since B is integral over A and C⊇A, b is integral over C. Let p(x)∈C[x] be the minimal polynomial of b. Since Ω is algebraically closed, the polynomial (σp)(x) has a root r in Ω. Consider the homomorphism ϕ:C[x]→Ω defined by q(x)↦(σq)(r). Let F be the fraction field of C, then F can be considered as subfield of Ω; and clearly (σp)(x) is also the minimal polynomial of b over F. So kerϕ=(p(x)), and ϕ induce an embedding from C[x]/(p(x))≃C[b] to Ω which restriction to σ on C. But C[b]⊋C, contradict the maximality of C.

□

Exercise 3. Let f:B→B′ be a homomorphism of A-algebras, and let C be an A-algebra. If f is integral, prove that f⊗1:B⊗A​C→B′⊗A​C is integral.

Proof. For any x⊗c∈B′⊗A​C, if xn+a1​xn−1+⋯+an​=0(ak​∈f(B))Then (x⊗c)n+(a1​⊗c)(x⊗c)n−1+⋯+(an​⊗cn)=0.So x⊗c is integral over f(B)⊗A​C. Since x⊗c generates B′⊗A​C, we have f⊗1 integral.

□

Exercise 4. Let A be a subring of a ring B such that B is integral over A. Let n be a maximal ideal of B and let m=n∩A be the corresponding maximal ideal of A. Is Bn​ necessarily integral over Am​?

Proof. No. Let A=k[x2−1] and B=k[x] (where k is a field), and let n=(x−1). Then m=n∩A=(x2−1). Since x2−(x2−1+1)=0, x is integral over A. Hence B is integral over A.

If 1/(x+1)∈Bn​ is integral over Am​, then 1/(x+1)n+a1​/(x+1)n−1+⋯+an​=0for some b1​,…,bn​∈Am​. Let h be the common denominator of b1​,…,bn​, and ak​=bk​h∈A. Then −(a1​+a2​(x+1)+⋯+an​(x+1)n−1)=h/(x+1).In particular, (x+1)∣h. But h∈/(x2−1), contradiction. So Bn​ is not integral over Am​.

□

Exercise 5. Let A⊂B be rings, B integral over A.

1.

If x∈A is a unit in B then it is a unit in A.

2.

The Jacobson radical of A is the contraction of the Jacobson radical of B.

Proof.

1.

If x is a unit in B, then x−1∈B is integral over A. Suppose x−n+a1​x−(n−1)+⋯+an​=0where a1​,…,an​∈A, then x−1=−(a1​+a2​x+⋯+an​xn−1).So x−1∈A, i.e. x is a unit in A.

2.

Since B integral over A, for any maximal ideal m of B, n=m∩A is a maximal ideal of A. Hence R(A)=n∈Max(A)⋂​n⊆m∈Max(B)⋂​(m∩A)=R(B)∩A.Conversely, if x∈R(B)∩A, then for any t∈A, 1+xt is a unit in B, hence by i) a unit in A. So x∈R(A). Thus R(A)=R(B)∩A.

□

Exercise 6. Let B1​,…,Bn​ be integral A-algebras. Show that ∏i=1n​Bi​ is an integral A-algebra.

Proof. For every k=1,…,n and every xk​∈Bk​, (1,1,…,xk​,1,…,1)∈∏i=1n​Bi​ is integral of A, since xk​ is integral over A. But every element of ∏i=1n​Bi​ is a finite product of such elements. So ∏i=1n​Bi​ is integral over A.

□

Exercise 7. Let A be a subring of a ring B, such that the set B−A is closed under multiplication. Show that A is integrally closed in B.

Proof. If u∈B is integral over A, choose a monic polynomial f∈A[x] of minimal degree such that f(u)=0. Suppose f(x)=xn+a1​xn−1+⋯+an​, then u(un−1+a1​un−2+⋯+an−1​)=−an​.We have −an​∈A, and by the choice of f, un−1+a1​un−2+⋯+an−1​∈B−A; since B−A is closed under multiplication, there must be u∈A. Hence A is integrally closed.

□

Exercise 8.

1.

Let A be a subring of an integral domain B, and let C be the integral closure of A in B. Let f,g be monic polynomials in B[x] such that fg∈C[x]. Then f,g are in C[x].

2.

Prove the same result without assuming that B (or A) is an integral domain.

Proof.

1.

Let K be the field of fraction of B, and L be the splitting field of fg over K. Then f,g split into linear factors in L. Suppose f=∏i=1n​(x−ui​), Then each ui​ is a root of the monic polynomial fg∈C[x], hence integral over C. Since the coefficients of f is polynomial of ui​’s, so integral over C. But C is integral closed in B, so f∈C[x]. Similarly we have g∈C[x].

2.

Similarly to i), it’s sufficient to show that there exists an extension D of B in which f splits into linear factors. Inductive on the degree of f. If degf=1 then there is nothing to prove. If degf>1, let D=B[t]/(f(t)), then B can embed into D, and tˉ∈D is a root of f; hence f(x)−(x−tˉ)f1​(x) for some f1​∈D[x]. By inductive hypothesis, there exists an extenstion L of D such that f1​ splits into linear factors, so is f.

□

Exercise 9. Let A be a subring of a ring B and let C be the integral closure of A in B. Prove that C[x] is the integral of A[x] in B[x].

Proof. If u is integral over A, then clearly uxk is integral over A[x] for any k. Hence C[x] is integral over A[x]. So it’s sufficient to show: if f(x) is integral over A[x], then f(x)∈C[x].

Suppose fm+g1​fm−1+⋯+gm​=0where gi​∈A[x]. Let r be an integer larger than m and the degrees of f,g1​,…,gm​. Let f1​=f−xr, then (f1​+xr)m+g1​(f1​+xr)m−1+⋯+gm​=0or say f1m​+h1​f1m−1​+⋯+hm​=0.And hm​=xrm+g1​xr(m−1)+⋯+gm​ is a monic polynomial. Let f2​=f1m−1​+h1​f1m−2​+⋯+hm−1​, then −f1​f2​=hm​, and −f1​, hm​ are both monic polynomials, so is f2​. Since hm​∈A[x]⊆C[x], by Exercise 8, −f1​∈C[x]. So f=xr+f1​∈C[x].

□

Exercise 10. A ring homomorphism f:A→B is said to have the going-up property (resp. the going-down property) if the conclusion of the going-up theorem (5.11)1 (resp. the going-down theorem (5.16)2) holds for B and its subring f(A).

Let f∗:Spec(B)→Spec(A) be the mapping associated with f.

1.

Consider the following three statements:

1.

f∗ is a closed mapping.

2.

f has the going-up property.

3.

Let q be any prime ideal of B and let p=qc. Then f∗:Spec(B/q)→Spec(A/p) is surjective.

Prove that (a) ⟹ (b) ⟺ (c). (See also Chapter 6, Exercise 11)

Proof.

(a) ⟹ (b):

If f∗ is closed, then for every prime ideal q of B, f∗(V(q)) is closed. Let p=f∗(q)=q∩A, then p∈f∗(V(q)), so V(p)={p}​⊆f∗(V(q)). Clearly this is equivalent to (b).

(b) ⟺ (c):

f has the going-up property ⟺ for every p=qc and for every p′⊇p, there exists q′⊇q such that p′=q′c ⟺ for every p=qc, the map f∗:Spec(B/q)→Spec(A/p) is surjective.

□

2.

Consider the following three statements:

1.

f∗ is a open mapping.

2.

f has the going-down property.

3.

Let q be any prime ideal of B, if p=qc. Then f∗:Spec(Bq​)→Spec(Ap​) is surjective.

Prove that (a’) ⟹ (b’) ⟺ (c’). (See also Chapter 7, Exercise 23)

Proof.

(a’) ⟹ (c’):

Let q be a prime ideal of B, and p=qc. By Chapter 3, Exercise 23, Bq​=t∈/qlim​​Bt​.So by Chapter 3, Exercise 26, f∗(Spec(Bq​))=t∈/q⋂​f∗(Spec(Bt​))=t∈/q⋂​f∗(Yt​).Since Yt​ is an open neighberhood of q, and f∗ is open, each f∗(Yt​) is an open neighberhood of p, thus contains Spec(Ap​). Hence f∗(Spec(Bq​))⊇Spec(Ap​).

(b’) ⟺ (c’):

f has the going-down property ⟺ for every p=qc and for every p′⊆p, there exists q′⊆q such that p′=q′c ⟺ for every p=qc, the map f∗:Spec(Bq​)→Spec(Ap​) is surjective.

□

Exercise 11. Let f:A→B be a flat homomorphism of rings. Then f has the going-down property.

Proof. By Chapter 3, Exercise 18, for every prime ideal q of B, let p=qc, then Spec(Bq​)→Spec(Ap​) is surjective. Hence by Exercise 10, f has the going-down propery.

□

Exercise 12. Let G be a finite group of automorphisms of the ring A, and let AG denote the subring of G-invariants, that is of all x∈A such that σ(x)=x for all σ∈G. Prove that A is integral over AG.

Proof. For every t∈A, let f(x)=∏σ∈G​(x−σ(t)). Then f(t)=0, and (σ0​(f))(x)=σ∈G∏​(x−σ0​(σ(t)))=σ′∈G∏​(x−σ′(t))=f(x).Hence f(x)∈AG[x]. So t is integral over AG.

□

Let S be a multiplicative closed subset of A such that σ(S)⊂S for all σ∈G, and let SG=S∩AG. Show that the action of G on A extends to an action on S−1A, and that (SG)−1AG≃(S−1A)G.

Proof. For every σ∈G,a∈A,s∈S, define σ(a/s)=σ(a)/σ(s). If a1​/s1​=a2​/s2​, i.e. exists t∈S such that t(a1​s2​−a2​s1​)=0, then t(σ(a1​)σ(s2​)−σ(a2​)σ(s1​))=0, so σ(a1​/s1​)=σ(a2​/s2​). Hence this extension is well-defined.

For every s∈SG,a∈AG, clearly a/s∈(S−1A)G; and this induces a homomorphism φ:(SG)−1AG→(S−1A)G. It’s easy to see φ is injective. Conversely, if a/s∈(S−1A)G, let s′=∏σ∈G​σ(s), and a′=a∏σ∈G,σ=1​σ(s), then s′∈SG, and a′/s′=a/s. So a′∈AG, and hence φ is surjective. Thus (SG)−1AG is isomorphic to (S−1A)G.

□

Exercise 13. In the situation of Exercise 12, let p be a prime ideal of AG, and let P be the set of prime ideals of A whose contraction is p. Show that G acts transitively on P. In particular, P is finite.

Proof. Clear if a prime ideal q of A satisfies qc=p, then for any σ∈G, (σq)c=p. Hence G acts on P.

For any two prime ideals q1​,q2​∈P, and for any x∈q1​, we have x′=∏σ∈G​σ(x)∈q1​∩AG=p. In particular, x′∈q2​, hence there exists σ∈G such that σ(x)∈q2​; that is, x∈σ−1(q2​). So q1​⊆⋃σ∈G​σ(q2​). Thus, q1​⊆σ(q2​) for some σ∈G.

By Cor 5.9, since A is integral over AG, and q1c​=(σ(q2​))c=p, we have q1​=σ(q2​). Hence G acts transitively on P.

□

Exercise 14. Let A be an integrally closed domain, K its field of fractions and L a finite normal separable extension of K. Let G be the Galois group of L over K and let B be the integral closure of A in L. Show that σ(B)=B for all σ∈G, and that A=BG.

Proof. If t∈B, then there exists monic polynomial f(x)∈A[x] such that f(t)=0. Suppose f(x)=xk+a1​xk+1+⋯+ak​, then for any σ∈G, we have f(σ(t))=σ(t)k+a1​σ(t)k−1+⋯+ak​=σ(tk+a1​tk−1+⋯+ak​)=σ(f(t))=0Thus σ(B)⊆B. Conversely σ−1(B)⊆B, i.e. B⊆σ(B). Hence σ(B)=B.

For the second proposition, we have BG=LG∩B=K∩B=A.

□

Exercise 15. Let A,K be as in Exercise 14, let L be any finite extension field of K, and let B be the integral closure of A in L. Show that, if p is any prime ideal of A, then the set of prime ideals q of B which contract to p is finite (in other words, that Spec(B)→Spec(A) has finite fibers).

Proof. Since every field extension can decompose to a separable extension and a purely inseparable extension, it reduce to this two cases.

If L/K is separable, then L can embed in a Galois extension E of K. Let C denote the integral closure of A in E, then by Exercise 14 we have CG=A. By Exercise 13, for any p, there exists finite prime ideals q of C, which contract to p. Since C is integral over B, every prime ideal of B is the contraction of some prime ideal of C. Thus there is only finite prime ideals of B which contract to p.

If L/K is purely inseparable, p is a prime ideal of A, q is a prime ideal of B such that q∩A=p, then for every x∈q, since L/K is purely inseparable, there exists m≥0 such that xpm∈K, so xpm∈K∩q=p. Conversely if xpm∈p⊆q, then x must belong to q. Hence q={x∈B∣∃m≥0,xpm∈p}is unique determined by p. In particular, Spec(B)→Spec(A) is bijective.

□

Noether’s normalization lemma

Exercise 16. Let k be a field and let A=0 be a finitely generated k-algebra. Then there exists elements y1​,…,yr​∈A which are algebraically independent over k and such that A is integral over k[y1​,…,yr​].

We shall assumen that k is infinite. (The result is still true if k is finite, but a different proof is needed.) Let x1​,…,xn​ generate A as k-algebra. We can renumber xi​ so that x1​,…,xr​ are algebraically independent over k and each of xr+1​,…,xn​ is algebraic over k[x1​,…,xr​]. Now proceedby induction on n. If n=r there is nothing to do, so suppose n>r and the result true for n−1 generators. The generator xn​ is algebraic over k[x1​,…,xn−1​], hence there exists a polynomial f=0 in n variables such that f(x1​,…,xn−1​,xn​)=0. Let F be the homogeneous part of highest degree in f. Since k is infinite, there exists λ1​,…,λk​ such that F(λ1​,…,λn−1​,1)=0. Put xi′​=xi​−λi​xn​ (1≤i≤n−1). Show that xn​ is integral over the ring A′=k[x1′​,…,xn−1′​], and hence that A is integral over A′. Then apply the inductive hypothesis to A′ to complete the proof.

Proof. Let g(x)=f(x1′​+λ1​x,…,xn−1′​+λn−1​x,x). Consider the leading coefficient of g, clearly it’s u=F(λ1​,λ2​,…,λn−1​,1)∈k. Hence u1​g(x)∈A′[x] is a monic polynomial in x, and g(xn​)=f(x1​,x2​,…,xn−1​,xn​)=0.So xn​ is algebraic over A′. Thus A is algebraic over A′. Then apply the inductive hypothesis to A′.

□

From the proof it follows that y1​,…,yr​ may be chosen to be linear combinations of x1​,…,xn​. This has the following geometrical interpretation: if k is algebraically closed and X is an affine algebraic variety in kn with coordinate ring A=0, then there exists a linear subspace L of dimension r in kn and a linear mapping of kn onto L which maps X onto L.

Proof. Let tˉk​ denote the image of tk​ in A=k[t1​,…,tn​]/P(X), then A is generated by tˉ1​,…,tˉn​. by the proposition above, since k is algebraically closed (so infinite), there exists algebraically independent y1​,…,yr​∈A, such that y1​,…,yr​ is linear combinations of tˉ1​,…,tˉn​; and A is integral over k[y1​,…,yr​].

Let L=kr, let yi​=∑j=1n​aij​tˉj​, then define φ:kn→L as φ(x)i​=j=1∑n​aij​xj​.Clearly φi​ restricts to yi​ on X.

To show that φ maps X onto L, let (a1​,…,ar​) be a given point of L. Since y1​,…,yr​ is algebraically independent, there exists a ring homomorphism p:k[y1​,…,yr​]→k such that p(yi​)=ai​. Since A is integral over k[y1​,…,yr​], by Exercise 2 p can extends to a homomorphism p~​:A→k, such that p~​(yi​)=ai​. Let x=(p~​(tˉ1​),…,p~​(tˉn​)), then clearly φ(x)=(a1​,…,ar​).

□

Nullstellensatz (weak form)

Exercise 17. Let X be an affine algebraic variety in kn, where k is an algebraically closed field, and let I(X) be the ideal of X in the polynomial ring k[t1​,…,kn​] (Chapter 1, Exercise 27). If I(X)=(1) then X is not empty.

Proof. If I(X)=1 then the coordinate ring A=k[t1​,…,tn​]/I(X) is not trivial. By Exercise 16, there exists r≥0 and a surjective map φ:X→kr. Hence X is nonempty.

□

Deduce that every maximal ideal in the ring k[t1​,…,tn​] is of the form (t1​−a1​,…,tn​−an​) where ai​∈k.

Proof. Let m be a maximal ideal of k[t1​,…,tn​], and let A=k[t1​,…,tn​]/m. Then A is a field. By Exercise 16, there exists algebraically independent y1​,…,yr​∈A, such that A is integral over k[y1​,…,yr​]. Since k[y1​,…,yr​] is an integral domain, and A is field, it follows that k[y1​,…,yr​] is a field, thus r=0. So A is integral over k, i.e. algebraic over k. Hence A=k, cause k is algebraic closed. Let ai​ denote the image of k[t1​,…,tn​] in A=k. Then clearly m=(t1​−a1​,…,tn​−an​)□

Exercise 18. Let k be a field and let B be a finitely generated k-algebra. Suppose that B is a field. Then B is a finite algebraic extension of k. (This is another version of Hilbert’s Nullstellensatz. The following proof is due to Zariski. For other proofs, see (5.24) (7.9).)

Let x1​,…,xn​ generate B as a k-algebra. The proof is by induction on n. If n=1 the result is clearly true, so assume n>1. Let A=k[x1​] and let K=k(x1​) be the field of fractions of A. By induction hypothesis, B is a finite algebraic extension of K, hence each x2​,…,xn​ satisfies a monic polynomial equation with coefficients in K, i.e. coefficients of the form a/b where a and b are in A. If f is the product of the denominatoes of all these coefficients, then each of x2​,…,xn​ is integral over Af​. Hence B and therefore K is integral over Af​.

Suppose x1​ is transcendental over k. Then A is integrally closed, because it is a unique factorization domain. Hence Af​ is integrally closed (5.12), and therefore Af​=K, which is clearly absurd. Hence x1​ is algebraic over k, hence K (and therefore B) is a finite extension of k.

Proof. If x1​ is transcendental over k, then A=k[x1​] is isomorphic to the polynomial ring over k, hence is a Euclid domain, hence a UFD.

If A is a UFD, K is its field of fractions, and u/v∈K is integral over A, then (u/v)n=∑k=0n−1​ak​(u/v)k, where ak​∈A. Assume u and v is coprime (i.e. (u)+(v)=(1)), then un=∑k=0n−1​ak​ukvn−k, hence un∈(v). But un is coprime with v, so v must be a unit, hence u/v∈A. Thus A is integrally closed.

If Af​=K is a field, let f=p1​p2​…pm​ be the unique factorization of f. Since A≃k[x] has infinite many prime elements, there exists a prime element p∈A distinct from p1​,…,pm​. Let u/fn be the invert of p in Af​, then pu=fn=p1n​…pmn​. This contradict the fact that A is UFD.

□

Exercise 19. Deduce the result of Exercise 17 from Exercise 18.

Proof. If m is a maximal ideal of k[t1​,…,tn​], then B=k[t1​,…,tn​]/mis a finitely generated k-algebra. By Exercise 18, B is a finite algebraic extension of k. But k is algebraically closed, hence B=k. Let ai​ be the image of ti​ in B=k, then ti​−ai​∈m, and clearly m=(t1​−a1​,…,tn​−an​).

□

Exercise 20. Let A be a subring of an integral domain B such that B is finitely generated over A. Show that there exists s=0 in A and elements y1​,…,yn​ in B, algebraically independent over A and such that Bs​ is integral over Bs′​, where B′=A[y1​,…,yn​].

Proof. Let S=A−0 and K=S−1A be the field of fractions of A. Then S−1B is finitely generated K-algebra, and therefore by Exercise 16, there exists x1​,…,xn​ in S−1B algebraically independent over K, and such that S−1B is integral over x1​,…,xn​.

Let z1​,…,zn​ generate B as A-algebra, then each of zi​ satisfies a monic polynomial with coefficients in K[x1​,…,xn​]. Suppose zimi​​=k=0∑mi​−1​aij​zik​where aij​∈K[x1​,…,xn​]. Since K=S−1A and xi​∈S−1B, there exists a common denominator s=0∈A of aij​ and xi​, such that yi​=sxi​∈B, and saij​∈A[y1​,…,yn​]=B′. Since xi​’s are algebraically independent over A, must also be yi​’s. By saij​∈B′, we know that aij​∈Bs′​. Hence each zi​ is integral over Bs′​. Since z1​,…,zn​ generate B as A-algebra, they must generate Bs​ as As​-algebra. So Bs​ is integral over Bs′​.

□

Exercise 21. Let A,B be as in Exercise 20. Show that there exists s=0 in A such that, if Ω is an algebraically closed field and f:A→Ω is a homomorphism for which f(s)=0, then f can be extended to a homomorphism B→Ω.

Proof. By Exercise 20, there exists algebraically independent y1​,…,yn​ in B and s=0 in A, such that Bs​ is integral over Bs′​, where B′=A[y1​,…,yn​]. For any f:A→Ω such that f(s)=0, first extend f to B′ (Since y1​,…,yn​ is algebraically independent, we can, for example, let f(y1​)=⋯=f(yn​)=0). Then since f(s)=0, f can be extended to Bs′​. By Exercise 2, Bs​ is integral over Bs′​, so f can be extended to Bs​, hence to B.

□

Exercise 22. Let A,B be as in Exercise 20. If the Jacobson radical of A is zero, then so is the Jacobson radical of B.

Proof. Let v=0 be an element of B, then we need to show there is a maximal ideal of B which does not contain v. Applying Exercise 21 to Bs​ with its subring A, we obtain s=0 in A. Let m be a maximal ideal of A which does not contain s, and k=A/m. Then the canonical mapping A→k extends to a homomorphism Bv​→Ω, where Ω is an algebraic closure of k. Its restriction g:B→Ω satisfies gB​(v)=0, so v∈/kergB​. It’s sufficient to show kerg is maximal. Since g(A)=k, and B is A-algebra, it follows g(B) is k-algebra. But g(B)⊂Ω is algebraic over k, hence g(B) is a field. So kerg is maximal.

□

Exercise 23. Let A be a ring. Show that the following are equivalent:

1.

Every prime ideal in A is an intersection of maximal ideals.

2.

In every homomorphic image of A the nilradical is equal to the Jacobson radical.

3.

Every prime ideal in A which is not maximal is equal to the intersection of the prime ideals which contain it strictly.

Proof.

i) ⟹ ii):

It’s sufficient to show in every A/a, ii) holds. In this case, every prime ideal containing a is an intersection of maximal ideals which containing a. Hence p⊇a⋂​p=m⊇a⋂​m.So in A/a the nilradical is equal to the Jacobson radical.

ii) ⟹ i):

For every prime ideal p of A, by ii), in A/p the nilradical is equal to the Jacobson radical. This means p is equal to the intersection of all maximal ideals of A which contains p.

i) ⟹ iii):

Obvious.

iii) ⟹ i):

If i) doesn’t hold, then there exists prime ideal p of A which is not the intersection of all maximal ideals containing it. Clearly if iii) holds for A then it holds for every quotient of A. So passing to the quotient ring, we may assume A is an integral domain, and the Jacobson radical is not zero.

Suppose f=0 in the Jacobson radical of A, then Af​=0 since A is a domain. Let m be a maximal ideal in Af​, and n be its contraction in A. Then f∈/n, and n is maximal with respect to this property. Since f is in the Jacobson radical, n can’t be maximal. But any prime ideal which contains n strictly must contain f, hence f∈p⊋n⋂​p.So n is not equal to the intersection of the prime ideals which contain it strictly.

□

A ring A with the three equivalent properties above is called a Jacobson ring.

Exercise 24. Let A be a Jacobson ring (Exercise 23) and B an A-algebra. Show that if B is either (i) integral over A or (ii) finitely generated as an A-algebra, then B is Jacobson.

In particular, every finitely generated ring, and every finitely generated algebra over a field, is a Jacobson ring.

Proof.

1.

For any prime ideal q of B, we must show the Jacobson radical in B/q is zero. Let B′ denote B/q, and A′ the image of A in B′. Then B′ is an integral domain, and since A is a Jacobson ring, the Jacobson radical of A′ is zero. Let R be the Jacobson radical of B′, then by Exercise 5, R∩A is equal the Jacobson radical of A′, so zero. If x=0∈R, suppose xn+a1​xn−1+⋯+an​=0where ak​∈A′, and we may assume an​=0 since B′ is an integral domain. Then an​∈R∩A, contradiction. So R=0, and B is a Jacobson ring.

2.

For any prime ideal q of B, let A′ and B′ be as in (i). Then B′ is an integral domain, so is A′. Hence in A′ the Jacobson radical is zero. By Exercise 22, since B′ is finitely generated over A′, so the Jacobson radical of B′ is also zero.

□

Exercise 25. Let A be a ring. Show that the following are equivalent:

1.

A is a Jacobson ring;

2.

Every finitely generated A-algebra B which is a field is finite over A.

Proof.

i) ⟹ ii):

By consider the image of A in B, we may assume that A is a subring of B. Let s=0∈A be in Exercise 21, then there exists a maximal ideal m of A which doesn’t contain s. Let k=A/m, and extend the canonical map A→k to B→Ω by Exercise 21, where Ω is an algebraically closure of k. Since B is a field and g(s)=0, g is injective. But m⊆kerg, so m=0, and A=k is a field. Since B can be embeded into the algebraic closure of A, it is algebraic over A. Thus B is finite over A.

ii) ⟹ i):

If p is a prime ideal of A which is not maximal, let B=A/p. Let f=0∈B, then Bf​ is finitely generated (by 1/f) over A. If it’s a field, then it’s finite over B, hence integral over B, therefore B is a field3, contradiction. So Bf​ is not a field. Let p be a maximal ideal of Bf​, and p′=p∩B. Then p′ is not zero and f∈/p′. Hence in B, the zero ideal is the intersection of all non-zero prime ideals. Thus A is Jacobson.

□

Exercise 26. Let X be a topological space. A subset of X is locally closed if it is the intersection of an open set and a closed set, or equivalently if it is open in its closure.

Proof. If L=U∩C, where U open and C closed, let C′=Lˉ denote its closure, then L⊆U∩C′. Also, L⊆C, hence C′⊆C. So U∩C′⊆U∩C=L. So L=U∩C′ is open in C′.

Conversely, if L is a subset such that L is open in its closure C=Lˉ, then by definition there exists an open subset U of X such that L=U∩C.

□

The following conditions on a subset X0​ of X are equivalent:

1.

Every non-empty locally closed subset of X meets X0​;

2.

For every closed set E in X we have E∩X0​​=E;

3.

The mapping U↦U∩X0​ of the collection of open sets of X onto the collection of open sets of X0​ is bijective.

A subset X0​ satisfying these conditions is said to be very dense in X.

Proof.

(1) ⟹ (2):

If E is a closed set and x∈E, then for each open neighborhood U of x, E∩U meets X0​, thus U meets E∩X0​. Hence x∈E∩X0​​, so E⊆E∩X0​​. Obviously E⊇E∩X0​​, hence E=E∩X0​​.

(2) ⟹ (3):

If U1​∩X0​=U2​∩X0​=L, let E1​=X−U1​,E2​=X−U2​, then E1​∩X0​=X0​−L=E2​∩X0​. Hence E1​=E1​∩X0​​=E2​∩X0​​=E2​, so U1​=U2​.

(3) ⟹ (1):

Let U,C be subsets of X open or closed respectively. If U∩C=∅, then U⊆V where V denotes X−C. Hence U∩X0​=(U∩V)∩X0​, thus U∩C∩X0​=(U−(U∩V))∩X0​=∅.

□

If A is a ring, show that the following are equivalent:

1.

A is a Jacobson ring;

2.

The set of maximal ideals of A is very dense in Spec(A);

3.

Every locally closed subset of Spec(A) consisting of a single point is closed.

Proof.

i) ⟺ ii):

For every C=V(a) closed, we will show C∩Max(A)​=C⟺ In A/a the nilradical is equal to the Jacobson radical. Let b=⋂{C∩Max(A)}, then C∩Max(A)​=V(b). Clearly b corresponds to the Jacobson radical in A/a, and r(a) corresponds to the nilradical in A/a. Hence C∩Max(A)​=C⟺V(b)=V(a)⟺b=r(a)⟺ the nilradical and the Jacobson radical in A/a are equal.

i) ⟺ iii):

If C∩D(b)={p}, write D(b) as D(b)=f∈b⋂​Xf​then there exists f∈b such that C∩Xf​={p}. Hence iii) is equivalent to: if C∩Xf​={p} where C closed, i.e. {p} is maximal in Xf​≃Spec(Af​), then p∈Max(A). This means if p is maximal along all prime ideals which containing f, then p is maximal. But there exists such f if and only if p is not the intersection of all prime ideals containing it strictly. Hence iii) is equivalent to i).

□

Valuation rings and valuations

Exercise 27. Let A,B be two local rings. B is said to be dominate A if A is a subring of B and the maximal ideal m of A is contained in the maximal ideal n of B (or, equivalently, if m=n∩A). Let K be a field and let Σ be the set of all local subrings of K. If Σ is ordered by the relation of denomination, show that Σ has maximal elements and that A∈Σ is maximal if and only if A is a valuation ring of K.

Proof. To show Σ has maximal elements, we should show it satisfies the conditions of Zorn’s lemma.

Clearly K∈Σ (with maximal ideal 0). Hence Σ is not empty. Let (Aα​) be a chain in Σ with maximal ideals mα​. Then A=⋃α​Aα​ is a subring of K, and clearly m=⋃α​mα​ is an ideal of A. So it’s sufficient to show m is the unique maximal ideal of A.

Let kα​=Aα​/mα​ be the residue field of Aα​, then the inclusion map Aα​↪Aβ​ (when α≤β) induces a embedding kα​↪kβ​ (since mα​⊆mβ​). Let k be the direct limit of the system (kα​), then by definition k is a field.

Since for any α≤β, we have the commutative diagram between the short exact sequence

[TikZ 编译错误]

By Chapter 2, Exercise 18, 19, there is a exact sequence 0→m→A→k→0.Hence m is a maximal ideal of A. If a is a ideal of A, then (a∩Aα​)⊆mα​ for any α, hence a⊆m. So m is the unique maximal ideal of A, and (A,m)∈Σ is an upperbound of the chain.

If A is a maximal element of Σ, let m be its maximal ideal and k its residue field. Let Ω be an algebraic closure of k. Then there is a canonical map φ:A→Ω. We finish this part with Theorem 5.214. Let Σ′ denote the poset as in Theorem 5.21, and (B,σ) an element in Σ′ such that (A,φ)≤(B,σ), that is, B⊇A is a subring and σ∣A​=φ. let n=kerσ, then n is a maximal ideal of B, and n∩A=m. Hence Bn​ dominate A, and by the maximality of (A,m) we know B=Bn​=A. Thus A is a maximal element in Σ′, hence a valuation ring of K.

On the other hand, if A is a valuation ring of K, then it’s local. Let m denote the maximal ideal of A. If (A,m) is dominated by (B,n), Then B⊇A is also a valuation ring of K. We must show that A=B. Let m0​=m−{0} and n0​=n−{0}. Then K−A=m0−1​ and K−B=n0−1​ (Here S−1={x−1∣x∈S}). Since m0​⊆n0​ we must have K−A⊆K−B. But A⊆B, hence A=B.

□

Exercise 28. Let A be an integral domain, K its field of fractions. Show that the following are equivalent:

1.

A is a valuation ring of K;

2.

If a,b are any two ideals of A, then either a⊆b or b⊆a.

Deduce that if A is a valuation ring and p is a prime ideal of A, then A/p and Ap​ are valuation rings of their fields of fractions.

Proof.

(1) ⟹ (2):

If a⊆b and b⊆a, then there exists x∈a−b and y∈b−a. Hence (x/y)∈/A and (y/x)∈/A, contradiction.

(2) ⟹ (1):

For any u,v∈A such that v=0, we have (u)⊆(v) or (v)⊆(u), hence (v/u)∈A or (u/v)∈A. So A is a valuation ring of K.

If A is a valuation ring, then it is a valuation ring of K, so it satisfies (2). Clearly A/p and Ap​ also satisfies (2), hence are valuation rings of their fields of fractions.

□

Exercise 29. Let A be a valuation ring of a field K. Show that every subring of K which contains A is a local ring of A.

Proof. If B⊇A is a subring of K, then let p⊆A as p={x∈A∣x−1∈/B}.We will show B=Ap​.

If x∈B−A, then x−1∈A−p. Hence x=(x−1)−1∈Ap​, so B⊆Ap​. Conversely, if x∈A,y∈A−p, then y−1∈B, and xy−1∈B, thus Ap​⊆B. Hence B=Ap​.

□

Exercise 30. Let A be a valuation ring of a field K. The group U of units of A is a subgroup of the multiplicative group K∗ of K.

Let Γ=K∗/U. If ξ,η∈Γ are represented by x,y∈K, define ξ≥η to mean xy−1∈A. Show that this defines a total ordering on Γ which is compatible with the group structure (i.e. ξ≥η⟹ξω≥ηω for all ω∈Γ). In other words, Γ is a totally ordered abelian group. It is called the value group of A.

Let v:K∗→Γ be the canonical homomorphism. Show that v(x+y)≥min(v(x),v(y)) for all x,y∈K∗.

Proof. First we show that ξ≥η is well defined. If ξ,η∈Γ is also represented by x′,y′∈K, then x′x−1 and yy′−1 are units in A, hence x′y′−1=(xy−1)(x′x−1)(yy′−1), and x′y′−1∈A if and only if xy−1∈A.

Clearly ≥ is transitive, reflexive. By the definition of Γ, it follows that ≥ is antisymmetric. Since A is a valuation ring, ≥ is connexive. Hence (Γ,≥) is a totally ordered set. For any ξ,η,ω∈Γ, if ξ,η,ω are represented by x,y,z, then xy−1∈A⟹(xz)(yz)−1∈A, hence ξ≥η⟹ξω≥ηω.

For all x,y∈K∗, by definition, v(x+y)≥min(v(x),v(y)) means v(x+y)≥v(x) or v(x+y)≥v(y), that is, (x+y)x−1∈A or (x+y)y−1∈A. Since A is a valuation ring, it’s clearly true.

□

Exercise 31. Conversely, let Γ be a totally ordered abelian group (written additively), and let K be a field. A valuation of K with values in Γ is a mapping v:K∗→Γ such that

1.

v(xy)=v(x)+v(y),

2.

v(x+y)≥min(v(x),v(y)),

for all x,y∈K∗. Show that the set of elements x∈K∗ such that v(x)≥0 is a valuation ring of K. This ring is called the valuation ring of v, and the subgroup v(K∗) of Γ is the value group of v.

Proof. Let A={x∈K∣x=0∨v(x)≥0}. Clearly v(1)=v(−1)=0, hence 1,−1∈A. Hence by (1) (2) we know A is a ring.

If x∈K−A, then v(x)<0, hence v(x−1)=−v(x)>0, so x−1∈A. Hence A is a valuation ring of K.

The kernel of the homomorphism v:K∗→Γ is all elements x such that v(x)=0, i.e. the units in A. Let U denote the group of units in A, then v(K∗)≃K∗/U. Hence the definition of value group here is equivalent to the definition in the last exercise.

□

Thus the concepts of valuation ring and valution are essentially equivalent.

Exercise 32. Let Γ be a totally ordered abelian group. A subgroup Δ of Γ is isolated in Γ if, whenever 0≤β≤α and α∈Δ, we have β∈Δ. Let A be a valuation ring of a field K, with value group Γ (Exercise 31). If p is a prime ideal of A, show that v(A−p) is the set of elements ≥0 in an isolated subgroup Δ of Γ, and that the mapping so defined of Spec(A) into the set of isolated subgroups of Γ is bijective.

If p is a prime ideal of A, what are the value groups of the valuation rings A/p,Ap​?

Proof. Let E=v(A−p). Since xy∈A−p if and only if x∈A−p and y∈A−p, if ξ,η∈E, then ξ+η∈E. If ξ≥η and ξ∈E, suppose they are represented by x,y, then x∈A−p,y∈A and xy−1∈A. Hence y∈A−p, hence η∈E. So clear Δ=E∪(−E) is an isolated subgroup of Γ.

From the proof we also know that v(xy)∈Δ⟹v(x)∈Δ; let y runs through all units in A, we have x∈p⟺v(x)∈/Δ. Hence p=A∩v−1(Γ−Δ). Thus it’s easy to show the mapping is bijective.

If p is a prime ideal of A, let Δ be the corresponding isolated subgroup of Γ, and let K′ be the field of fractions of A/p. Consider the valuation φ:K′∗y+px+p​​→Δ↦v(x)−v(y)(x,y∈/p)​If x∈/p and x−x′∈p, then v(x)∈Δ,v(x−x′)∈/Δ. Since Δ is isolated, we have v(x−x′)>v(x), and so v(x)=v(x′). Hence the homomorphism is well-defined; and it’s clearly sujective. Also v\mleft(y+px+p​\mright)≥0 if and only if v(x)≥v(y), so equivalent to that there exists t∈A such that y+px+p​=t+p. Hence A/p is the valuation ring of this valuation. So Δ is the valuation group of A/p.

For Ap​, clearly its field of fraction is also K. By definition, its valuation group is K∗/U′, where U′ is the units in Ap​; that is, all x∈K such that v(x)∈/v(p), i.e. v(x)∈Δ. Hence the valuation group of Ap​ is Γ/Δ.

□

Exercise 33. Let Γ be a totally ordered abelian group. We shall show how to construct a field K and a valuation v of K with Γ as value group. Let k be any field and let A=k[Γ] be the group algebra over k. By definition, A is freely generated as a k-vector space by elements xα​(α∈Γ) such that xα​xβ​=xα+β​. Show that A is an integral domain.

Proof. If u=λ1​xα1​​+⋯+λn​xαn​​=0 and v=μ1​yβ1​​+⋯+μm​yβm​​=0, where λi​,μj​∈k−{0}, assume that α1​<α2​,…,αn​ and β1​<β2​,…,βm​. Then uv=λ1​μ1​xα1​+β1​​+i=1 or j=1∑​λi​μj​xαi​+βj​​.If i=1 or j=1, then αi​+βj​>αi​+βj​. Hence uv=0, so A is an integral domain.

□

If u=λ1​xα1​​+⋯+λn​xαn​​ is any non-zero elements of A, where the λi​ are all =0 and α1​<α2​<⋯<αn​, define v0​(u) to be α1​. Show that the mapping v0​:A−{0}→Γ satisfies conditions (1) and (2) of Exercise 31.

Proof.

1.

v0​(xy)=v0​(x)v0​(y): Same as the proof above.

2.

v0​(x+y)≥min(v0​(x),v0​(y)): Trivial.

□

Let K be the field of fractions of A. Show that v0​ can be unique extend to a valuation of K, and that the value group of v is precisely Γ.

Proof. By the property (1) of valuations, we must define v:K∗→Γ by v(x/y)=v0​(x)−v0​(y)(x,y∈A−{0}).Also we have v(x1​/y1​+x2​/y2​)​=v0​(x1​y2​+x2​y1​)−v0​(y1​y2​)≥min(v0​(x1​)+v0​(y2​),v0​(x2​)+v0​(y1​))−(v0​(y1​)+v0​(y2​))=min(v(x1​/y1​),v(x2​/y2​)).​So v is a valuation of K.

Clearly v:K∗→Γ is surjective, since v(xα​/1)=α. Hence the value group of v is precisely Γ.

□

Exercise 34. Let A be a valuation ring and K its field of fractions. Let f:A→B be a ring homomorphism such that f∗:Spec(B)→Spec(A) is a closed mapping. Then if g:B→K is any A-algebra homomorphism (i.e. if g∘f is the embedding of A in K) we have g(B)=A.

Proof. Let C=g(B), then C⊇A.

Let n be a maximal ideal of C, then since f∗ is closed, m=n∩A is the maximal ideal of A (since m=f∗(n), and {n} is closed). Hence Cm​ dominate A, so by Exercise 27, Cm​=A. Thus C⊆A.

□

Exercise 35. From Exercises 1 and 3 it follows that, if f:A→B is integral and C is any A-algebra, then the mapping (f⊗1)∗:Spec(B⊗A​C)→Spec(C) is a closed map.

Proof. By Exercise 3, f⊗1:A⊗A​C(=C)→B⊗A​C is also integral; then by Exercise 1, (f⊗1)∗ is closed.

□

Conversely, suppose that f:A→B has this property and that B is an integral domain. Then f is integral.

Proof. Let A1​=f(A)⊆A and i:A1​→B be the inclusion map. Then for any A1​-algebra C, consider (i⊗1)∗:Spec(B⊗A1​​C)→Spec(C)It’s same as (f⊗1)∗:Spec(B⊗A​C)→Spec(C), so integral. Hence we can replace A with A1​, assume that A⊆B and f be the inclusion map.

Let K be the field of fractions of B and let A′ be a valuation ring of K containing A. By Corollary 5.225 we have to show B⊆A′. By hypothesis, Spec(B⊗A​A′)→Spec(A′) is a closed map. Apply Exercise 34 to the homomorphism B⊗A​A′→K defined by b⊗a′↦ba′. Then it follows that ba′∈A′ for any b∈B and a′∈A′. By taking a′=1, we have B⊆A′.

□

Show that the result just proved remains valid if B is a ring with only finitely many minimal prime ideals (e.g. if B is Noetherian).

Proof. Let p1​,…,pn​ be the minimal prime ideals of B, and let fi​:A→B/pi​ be the composition of A→B→B/pi​. For any A-algebra C, clear Spec(B/pi​⊗C)→Spec(B⊗C) is closed, so Spec(B/pi​⊗C)→Spec(C) is closed by hypothesis.

Apply the result above, each fi​:A→B/pi​ is integral. Hence A→∏i=1n​(B/pi​) is integral. Since ∏i=1n​(B/pi​)≃B/⋂i=1n​pi​=B/R by the Chinese remainder theorem (where R is the nilradical of B), it follows that B/R is integral over A. Thus B is integral over A (if f(x+R)=R, then f(x)∈R, hence f(x)k=0 for some k).

□
